stem: Congenital hydrocephalus is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: DW causes hydrocephalus but is not the most common cause.

Correct! Aqueductal stenosis (narrowing of the cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius) is the most common cause of congenital hydrocephalus. Causes obstructive (non-communicating) hydrocephalus — CSF cannot flow from 3rd to 4th ventricle. Can be X-linked (L1CAM mutation). Presents with macrocephaly, bulging fontanelle, sunset eyes (upgaze palsy). Treatment: ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt or endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV).
CP papilloma causes hydrocephalus by CSF overproduction but is rare.
Chiari I may cause syringomyelia but is not the most common cause of congenital hydrocephalus.

explanation:
Aqueductal stenosis: most common cause of congenital hydrocephalus. Non-communicating. Can be X-linked (L1CAM). Treatment: VP shunt or ETV.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK560909] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560909/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A newborn with seizures has MRI showing a smooth brain lacking normal gyri and sulci. Which disorder is this?
explanation: Lissencephaly ('smooth brain') results from defective neuronal migration during weeks 12-24. LIS1 (Miller-Dieker, chr 17) and DCX (X-linked) mutations are most common. Presents with severe developmental delay and seizures. [StatPearls NBK560766]

feedback.correct: Correct! Lissencephaly = smooth brain from defective neuronal migration.
feedback.incorrect: Smooth brain surface = lissencephaly, the classic neuronal migration disorder.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: An adolescent has occipital headaches worsened by Valsalva, with MRI showing cerebellar tonsils herniated >5 mm below the foramen magnum. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Chiari I malformation = cerebellar tonsil herniation >5 mm below the foramen magnum. Symptoms include occipital headaches worsened by coughing/straining/Valsalva, and may include syringomyelia (25-65%). Chiari II involves cerebellar vermis herniation AND is strongly associated with myelomeningocele. Chiari I is often acquired/developmental; Chiari II results from failed neural tube closure drawing the hindbrain downward. [StatPearls NBK448076]

feedback.correct: Correct! Tonsils >5 mm below foramen magnum + Valsalva headaches = Chiari I. Chiari II involves vermis + myelomeningocele.
feedback.incorrect: Chiari I = tonsil herniation, Valsalva headaches. Chiari II = vermis herniation + myelomeningocele.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Which Chiari malformation is strongly associated with myelomeningocele and involves herniation of the cerebellar vermis?
explanation: Chiari II (Arnold-Chiari) involves downward herniation of the cerebellar vermis, brainstem, and fourth ventricle through the foramen magnum. It is virtually always associated with myelomeningocele (open spinal dysraphism). The 'open' neural tube defect allows CSF leak, reducing posterior fossa volume and causing hindbrain herniation. Hydrocephalus develops in ~90%. [StatPearls NBK448076]

feedback.correct: Correct! Chiari II = vermis + brainstem herniation + myelomeningocele. Hydrocephalus in ~90%.
feedback.incorrect: Chiari II always associates with myelomeningocele. The open NTD → CSF leak → small posterior fossa → herniation.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: What spinal cord condition commonly develops as a complication of Chiari I malformation?
explanation: Syringomyelia (fluid-filled cavity within the spinal cord) develops in 25-65% of Chiari I patients due to disrupted CSF flow at the foramen magnum. Classic presentation: cape-like loss of pain and temperature sensation (crossing spinothalamic fibers damaged first) with preserved fine touch. Posterior fossa decompression surgery treats both the Chiari malformation and associated syrinx. [StatPearls NBK448076]

feedback.correct: Correct! Syringomyelia from obstructed CSF flow. Classic cape-like dissociated sensory loss.
feedback.incorrect: Chiari I → blocked CSF → syrinx → cape-like pain/temp loss with preserved touch (dissociated sensory loss).

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use
